nuggets.summary: When Dan Lewis quit Amazon to explore founding Convoy, he had two candidate ideas (trucking logistics and mobile retail) and wanted to validate them as fast as possible before raising money. For the trucking idea, he cold-messaged every trucking-industry contact he could find on LinkedIn, and each conversation led to introductions to three more people. He also physically went to warehouses and truck stops (in Marysville and elsewhere) to talk to truckers between their truck and the diner about what they liked and disliked about their work, and was kicked out of a Whole Foods warehouse in SoDo for showing up unannounced. This hands-on, in-person guerrilla approach — not surveys or secondary research — is what let him build a credible narrative before he had hired any engineers or built any product.
nuggets.quote: So, that was just honestly walking into warehouses. I went on LinkedIn and looked up every single person in the trucking industry and cold in-mailed them. And then they would talk to me and they'd introduce me to three other people. Kept doing that. Got kicked out of the Whole Foods warehouse down in Soto.

nuggets.summary: When validating his idea before founding Convoy, Dan Lewis deliberately avoided talking to friends about it, because friends will lie to make you feel good rather than give honest feedback — the opposite of what a founder actually needs at that stage. Instead, he sought out complete strangers (truckers, warehouse workers) who had no social incentive to flatter him. This reverses the common early-stage instinct to test an idea on your own network first; he treats friends as actively bad sources of validation, and strangers as the better source precisely because they have nothing invested in sparing your feelings.
nuggets.quote: Because the thing is, you shouldn't talk to your friends. You should talk to people that don't know you, because friends will lie to you to be nice to you. So friends are bad, bad customer validation. Don't talk to your friends. Talk to your non-friends.

nuggets.summary: The truckers and warehouse workers Dan Lewis interviewed for early customer research before Convoy existed later became actual paying customers once the product was built. He describes this as making the eventual sales conversation easier, because he could return to people who remembered him as the guy who'd cold-approached them at a truck stop and show them the finished product rather than cold-pitching a stranger from scratch. The unglamorous early research phase doubled as a warm pipeline for the company's first customers.
nuggets.quote: Those people I talked to early on became customers later, which is great. Because then you can go back to them and say, hey, man, you're so hopeful to me... Why don't I show you what we actually built? Oh, it works, cool. And then, you know, it's an easier sell than actually trying to sell them.

nuggets.summary: After spending six weeks doing hands-on customer research before he had any real product or hard data, Dan Lewis found that having concrete anecdotes from roughly a hundred real conversations completely changed his fundraising pitch, even without metrics to back numbers up. Whenever an investor pushed on a claim, he could answer with a specific story from an actual trucker or warehouse worker rather than a hypothesis. He credits this shift — leading with anecdote instead of hypothesis — with transforming how investor conversations went, well before Convoy had any data to show.
nuggets.quote: It changed the entire conversation from I have an idea to every time there was a question, I could pull an anecdote. I didn't have data yet, but I had anecdotes. And that changed all of the fundraising conversations.

nuggets.summary: At 22, deciding between becoming a Spanish teacher or going into management consulting, Dan Lewis got advice from a Yale dean about "option value": choosing the path that keeps more future paths open. The dean's reasoning was that starting in consulting keeps the door open to teaching later (high option value), but starting as a teacher makes it much harder to later break into consulting (low option value). When uncertain between two paths, the dean's advice was to pick the one with more option value — a specific decision heuristic Lewis says was the actual deciding factor, and one that generalizes well beyond this one career choice.
nuggets.quote: If you go do management consulting right now, you can have, the odds of you being able to go be a teacher later are really high. If you go do a teaching right now, the odds of being able to go into management consulting later are really low. So, if you're not sure which one you want to do, pick the one that has more option value.

nuggets.summary: Reflecting on becoming a first-time CEO, Dan Lewis says he arrived at the belief that humans are fundamentally limited in their capacity for empathy — you cannot actually understand what an experience is like (having kids, becoming a CEO) until you go through it yourself, no matter how much you've observed or been told about it in advance. He treats this as a general epistemic limit, not something specific to startups, and it changed how he thinks about trying to predict or prepare for major life and career transitions before living them.
nuggets.quote: I don't think humans are very good at empathy. I don't think you can actually understand what someone's really going through until you do it yourself in any category... I just kind of come to the conclusion now that you can't actually predict things. You don't know what they feel like until you do them.

nuggets.summary: Asked about work-life balance as a startup CEO, Dan Lewis says the real difficulty isn't the number of hours worked — he'd put in similarly long hours at prior jobs, including management consulting and large tech companies — but the fact that as CEO he's mentally "on" even when away from work, and struggles to disengage attention rather than time. He reframes the balance problem specifically as an attention-management problem rather than a time-management one, which points toward a different fix: building real distractions and focus elsewhere, not just blocking off hours on a calendar.
nuggets.quote: the hardest, it's not really about time, it's about the attention. How do you not attend to that when you're not at work, right?... Mentally processing things. Like, how do you actually let that go and then just focus on other things? So that's probably the harder part.

nuggets.summary: In parallel with the trucking research, Dan Lewis tested a second idea (mobile retail targeted at college students) using a similarly scrappy, low-cost method: he bought a stack of $5 Starbucks gift cards, made a paper questionnaire on a clipboard, dressed deliberately so he wouldn't "look sketchy," and went to community colleges to interview students in person about how they shopped on their phones — walking through their actual apps with them rather than asking hypothetical questions. The small cash-value incentive was enough to get strangers to sit for a structured interview, illustrating a cheap, repeatable way to get real behavioral evidence instead of guesses.
nuggets.quote: I went and bought a bunch of Starbucks $5 gift cards, got a clipboard, printed, like, a questionnaire, put on a shirt that didn't make me look sketchy. And then went to a bunch of community colleges and just interviewed tons of students... How do you shop on your phone? Walk me through the apps you use and just kept doing that.

nuggets.summary: Before Convoy had a finished product, Dan Lewis set a concrete numeric goal: reach 100 shipments, purely so the company would have enough real operating data to tell investors a credible story for a Series A raise. Rather than rallying the team around an abstract mission, this specific, countable target created urgency and alignment, and it directly enabled an aggressive fundraising timeline — going from company founding to a credible Series A pitch in about eight months. The insight is that a concrete, even somewhat arbitrary, numeric milestone motivates a team and drives execution in a way a vague mission statement doesn't.
nuggets.quote: we're, we're in trucking. We were new. Like, we need a hundred shipments, um, by this date. We didn't have a product. Like, we need to get a hundred shipments at least so we can understand our business enough to go have a really credible story about how to take it to the next level.

nuggets.summary: Many startups try to write their company values on day one. Dan Lewis asked Jeff Bezos, a Convoy investor, when and how Amazon created its leadership principles, expecting an early foundational exercise. Instead, Bezos said Amazon deliberately waited until the company had grown to roughly 90-100 people, then looked back at what had actually made them successful and only then codified those observed behaviors into formal values — rather than inventing aspirational values upfront and hoping the culture would conform to them. Convoy copied this approach directly, waiting about a year before drafting its own values based on what had genuinely worked for the business rather than what sounded good early on.
nuggets.quote: we didn't just go set the values. We, the company developed and then we saw what was working, right? And then we thought about, okay, what's working, now let's capture those and encapsulate those values into a set of things that we actually live by.

nuggets.summary: At Amazon, meetings don't use slide decks. Instead someone writes a six-page narrative Word document, and when the group convenes, the first roughly 30 minutes of a one-hour meeting are spent reading the document in silence before any discussion begins — the remaining time is spent debating the actual substance rather than sitting through a scripted pitch. Dan Lewis's takeaway is that a narrative document is far harder to "mask" weak thinking behind than a PowerPoint, where formatting and bullet points can paper over gaps in logic, so the format itself forces higher-quality, more transparent thinking rather than just being a stylistic preference.
nuggets.quote: they don't do PowerPoints, right? It's a Word document. So you walk in, it's the six-pager, you write a document, you walk in, it reads it in silence before the meeting starts... You can mask things in a PowerPoint, you can mask things in a presentation, you can't mask them in a Word document.

nuggets.summary: At most companies, individual team goals get rolled up and aggregated as they move up the org chart — Dan Lewis cites Microsoft as an example, where seven team goals roll into one higher goal, several of those roll into another, and by the time it reaches the CEO he's tracking only 15-20 blended goals. Amazon does the opposite: goals from small teams of 10-20 people flow all the way to the top without aggregation, so senior leadership is literally tracking hundreds of individual team goals rather than a compressed summary. The counterintuitive effect is that teams feel more accountable, not less, because they know a specific senior leader could actually be watching their exact number rather than a diluted rollup that no one individual owns.
nuggets.quote: the goals that a lot of individual teams have, maybe a 10-person team or a 20-person team has, roll all the way up to the top. They don't get aggregated... at Microsoft, for example... these seven goals were rolling to this goal. And then... the CEO would have like 15 or 20 goals. And so there was kind of a watered-down effect.

nuggets.summary: Dan uses Amazon's reviews team as a cautionary example of how solving a problem can accidentally erase the problem itself. Amazon's original problem was customer confidence in buying without touching a product; the solution was reviews, but the team was literally named 'the reviews team,' so every hire and goal became 'make reviews go up and to the right' rather than 'increase customer confidence.' That narrowed focus meant Amazon almost missed other solutions to the same underlying problem, like letting customers ask other buyers questions or see fit information, and only unlocked those once someone forced the team to revisit the original problem instead of the solution. The takeaway he applies at Convoy is that naming a team or workstream after its current solution silently locks in tunnel vision, so it's worth periodically forcing a return to the original problem statement.
nuggets.quote: But then they called the team reviews. And then everyone they ever hired from there on out was hired to the reviews team. And so every goal they set was how do we make reviews go up and to the right?... And so if you forget about the problem you're originally solving and you focus on improving the solution, you may actually miss like five other amazing opportunities.

nuggets.summary: Building on the Amazon example, Dan says he deliberately tries to install a specific pushback habit into Convoy's culture: he wants employees to feel free to interrupt him mid-solution and ask what the original problem was and whether the current solution is actually still solving it. He frames this as something he wants directed at himself personally when he's 'going down on some solution,' not just a general principle, and admits it's hard to actually build into a company's culture rather than just state as a value. The actionable piece is narrow and concrete: it's a specific question ('what was the problem you started with? Are you sure this solves it?') meant to be asked routinely, not a vague call for more first-principles thinking.
nuggets.quote: we need to remember it's important for people in the company to push back and say, I think we're too obsessed with this solution right now... And if you, like I want people to ask me that. If I'm like going down on some solution, like, damn, what was the problem? Are you sure this solved the problem?

nuggets.summary: Convoy assumed truck drivers could be handled like Uber drivers and simply given a company phone if they didn't already have the right OS, but the model failed because trucking isn't a real-time job the way ride-hailing is. About 80-85% of drivers already had Android phones, so Convoy built Android-only at first and planned to hand out Android devices to the roughly 15-20% with iPhones. The flaw was behavioral, not technical: an Uber driver is glued to their phone constantly between jobs, while a trucker gets a notification about tomorrow's job while still doing today's, so they default to their own phone for real-time calls with dispatchers and only check the Convoy device at the end of the day. Realizing this too late meant engineering effort was misallocated before they pivoted quickly to build an iOS app.
nuggets.quote: we assumed we could give drivers phones... it was maybe 80-ish, 85% of truck drivers had smartphones... an Uber driver is always focused on their phone 100% of the time... Whereas a trucker is doing a job right now, but getting a notification for a job that starts tomorrow from convoy... they're not going to look at the other phone until the end of the day, because it's not real-time

nuggets.summary: Convoy's MVP built a real product on only one side of each two-sided relationship instead of building everything: an app for truck drivers/carriers but a manual back end for shippers, and a web interface for shippers but manual handling for carriers on the web side. In hindsight, Dan says he might have gone even further and picked just one single thing to build with technology (e.g., only the carrier experience) and done the rest by hand, because it's fine to run a business manually up front to validate the scenario before committing engineering and management bandwidth. His generalizable advice: identify the two most important steps in a multi-step transaction, build only those really well first, and do the rest manually so you don't fragment limited engineering and management bandwidth across too many problems at once.
nuggets.quote: We didn't go build a web experience for trucking companies, and we didn't build an app for shippers... it's completely okay to use manual solutions to a problem up front to make sure you've nailed the scenarios, and to not fragment your management bandwidth and your engineering bandwidth across too many problems at once. So pick... the two, maybe, that are the most important, and build those really well up front... and do the other ones manually

nuggets.summary: When raising Convoy's angel/seed round, Dan says investors are evaluating three specific things, not just the idea: first, whether the founder is fully committed and already working on it (not still employed elsewhere weighing whether to jump), because a founder who isn't already doing it is a much harder sell; second, whether the opportunity itself is credible, which is where storytelling matters most since most investors aren't domain experts; and third, and hardest, whether the founder can actually execute, i.e., can they assemble a team, or will the money just sit there with no one able to build it. He frames the third point as the one founders most often fail to de-risk, since it's easy to quit a job and tell a compelling story but much harder to prove you can already build the team.
nuggets.quote: there are three things that really matter. One is the person you're speaking with has to believe that you are passionate about it you're committed to it... Two is the opportunity obviously matters... What's hard often is can you go do it? Do you have four engineers or two engineers committed and working on this right now with you or if I give you the money is there now risk you're never going to be able to go hire the team to deliver on this?

nuggets.summary: To de-risk the 'can you actually execute' concern before raising money, Dan recruited five or six engineers to work on Convoy part-time and unpaid, before the seed round closed, rather than waiting until funding to build a team. He says this was more decisive for investors than anything else, more so than the deck or the story, because it directly proved execution risk was already being solved: the team's names, pictures, and willingness to serve as references were on the pitch materials. The lesson is that founders should treat assembling a partial, working team as part of the fundraising deliverable itself, not something to defer until after money is raised.
nuggets.quote: we had five or six people working on this who were engineers and helping us when we wanted to go raise money that was the thing that made all the difference the other stuff I mentioned is super important having the engineering team doing it and committed and like their names on a slide and their pictures and like them willing to be references changed it so that was that was pretty big

nuggets.summary: Dan argues founders should classify their business as 'linear' versus 'binary/scale-or-die' when pitching investors, and that this framing matters more than the sector's popularity. Binary businesses like gaming, messaging, or certain social networks only work if they hit massive scale or go viral — there's no reliable middle outcome, and mid-sized versions of these businesses (he cites Path, which reached medium scale but still didn't really succeed) essentially fail. Linear businesses like trucking/logistics instead convert effort and homework into proportional results, so Dan explicitly pitched Convoy to investors as a lower-risk, effort-driven bet rather than a lottery-ticket bet requiring a viral spark, which he believes made it feel safer to investors who didn't know the industry.
nuggets.quote: is this a linear business or a nonlinear business is this a binary business or is this a business that has sort of a gradient... there are a lot of businesses that really aren't going to be successful unless they're massive... whereas there are other businesses that are much more linear and effort and I think they're less risky... trucking for example and logistics I felt like it was less risky because I felt like we could put the energy in and do the homework

nuggets.summary: Convoy's founders explicitly ruled out bootstrapping because they believed trucking had just hit a narrow technology-enabled window: truck driver smartphone penetration, reliable data plans, and accurate GPS (even in rural areas) had only recently become widespread enough to make incumbents vulnerable to disruption. Because it was a marketplace business, they believed first-mover advantage and speed to market mattered enormously, so they treated time as directly convertible into money - raising capital was the way to buy the speed needed to capture the window before someone else did, rather than growing slowly on bootstrapped revenue.
nuggets.quote: we looked at this business as hey technology just shifted truck drivers didn't have smartphones two years ago... because technology allowed that to happen it actually in our business it's going to fundamentally change the way the entire business can be constructed... so we basically thought time equals money to accelerate us getting to market

nuggets.summary: Lewis compares picking investors to dating: choose people you genuinely enjoy talking to and feel natural chemistry with, because the moment they sign the first check is one of the best moments that relationship will ever have. From there the relationship generally gets harder, so the openness and trust built early is what founders end up leaning on later when things aren't going well - not something that can be built after the fact, once it's actually needed.
nuggets.quote: you've got to pick the right investors you have to like actually it's dating like when you meet the person you really want to actually enjoy the conversation you have with them and feel like there's some natural relationship and chemistry there because you're in the best of times when you're raising money from them for the first time... it only gets harder from the time they're signing that first check

nuggets.summary: Right after closing Convoy's seed round, some of its own investors advised Lewis to immediately write the Series B deck, even though the company hadn't yet raised its Series A - the logic being that if you believe you'll eventually raise that later round, you may never actually get there unless you define the story for it now. Convoy applied the same logic after the seed: they worked backward from what the Series A story needed to say (customer diversity across categories, enough shipment volume to understand the different permutations, key team gaps filled) to figure out exactly which milestones to hit before raising, rather than raising first and figuring out the story later.
nuggets.quote: some of the best advice I got even when we were raising our series A as soon as we were done some of our investors when I was talking about it they said write your series B deck right now like if you believe you're going to do a series B you may never do it

nuggets.summary: To build toward Convoy's Series A story, Lewis drew a two-by-two grid mapping the credibility areas the company needed: understanding the demand side of the marketplace, the supply side, marketplace dynamics generally, and the trucking industry itself. He used the grid as a live recruiting tracker, writing the name of every candidate he was interviewing into the corresponding box, and built the operations team in parallel with the product over one summer so that by the September launch - and by the time he pitched Series A investors - he had people in place who made the company's story credible in exactly the areas where he personally lacked deep experience.
nuggets.quote: I actually drew a grid it was like a two by two I was like I need to have people who understand you know demand side of the business in a marketplace the supply part of business in the marketplace really understand marketplace dynamics and really understand the trucking industry... I took every person I was speaking with during my recruiting during the summer... and we wrote everyone's name that we were recruiting on that sheet

nuggets.summary: Instead of defining company values at founding, Convoy's first five engineers and Lewis held a values retreat in week one and deliberately concluded they didn't yet know who they were as a company, so they paused the exercise entirely. About a year later, once the company had grown to roughly 30 people and Lewis could still see and talk to everyone end to end, they revisited it - this time by asking what behaviors they were already doing that were working and wanted to keep, rather than aspirationally inventing values from nothing. This retrospective, evidence-based approach to defining culture runs counter to the common startup advice to nail down values on day one.
nuggets.quote: we first started the company the first five engineers and me and Grant went to like a retreat for a weekend this is week one just to kind of talk about who we wanted to be as a company and we started thinking about values and goals and we kind of said we don't know who we are yet right we paused that and then a year later we came back to it and said what allowed us to achieve these things

nuggets.summary: One of Convoy's core values is "know why," which Lewis explains exists to enable employees to exercise independent judgment and handle situations not explicitly covered by instructions. His reasoning: if people only follow directives without understanding the underlying reason, they get stuck the moment circumstances change or the decision-maker isn't around to ask; understanding why also makes people more fulfilled at work because they can act autonomously instead of needing to be told what to do in every new situation.
nuggets.quote: know why is one of the things that plays into that because I don't think you can exercise independent judgment and you can't go off script and you can't handle exceptions and things like that if you don't know why you're doing something so I never want to have a culture where people are like well we're doing because Dan said so

nuggets.summary: Before starting Convoy, Lewis ran a structured idea-validation process: first extensive desk research (industry reports, competitor material), then forcing himself to write out every possible customer type in the industry and what each currently pays money for (trucks, software, advice, etc.), combining those into several candidate business concepts written up as short user-story-style paragraphs. Only after that did he go have open-ended field conversations with industry people, using the pre-written concepts to give the conversations enough shape to be useful without being overwhelming. That process produced three genuinely distinct trucking business ideas, none of which matched what he had originally written down - the field conversations reshaped the ideas rather than simply confirming them.
nuggets.quote: I actually did a bunch of research first right I went on the internet and learned everything I could about it... I forced myself to basically write down like who are the customers who are all the different customer options... and then wrote actual like paragraph or two... and so it didn't turn out to be any of the things we wrote down but there were actually three distinct businesses we came up with within trucking
